title: Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP)
oneLiner: Metastatic malignancy with no identifiable primary site after standard workup; ~3% of cancers, median survival ~6–12 months, but subsets are treatable.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old man presents with 4 weeks of progressive right upper quadrant pain, early satiety, and 5 kg weight loss. He has a 50‑pack‑year smoking history. Examination reveals hepatomegaly, mild jaundice, and a palpable left supraclavicular node. Bloods show deranged LFTs, LDH 850 U/L, and CEA 45 ng/mL. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrates multiple hypodense liver lesions, scattered lung nodules, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy but no primary tumour. Biopsy of the liver lesion shows poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) is CK7+/CK20–, TTF‑1 negative, GATA‑3 negative. A PET‑CT reveals no occult primary.

**Dissect the vignette – what each clue tells you:**

| Clue | Pattern Interpretation |
|------|------------------------|
| Age >65, smoker | High risk for lung, pancreas, head & neck primaries |
| Hepatomegaly + left supraclavicular node | Distant metastases (Virchow’s node often left‑sided from gastrointestinal primaries) |
| Multiple liver & lung mets | Haematogenous spread; primary could be lung, pancreas, colorectal, or breast |
| Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma | Most common histology in CUP (≈50%); need IHC to narrow origin |
| CK7+/CK20–, TTF‑1–, GATA‑3– | Favours lung (but TTF‑1 negative argues against classic lung adenocarcinoma), pancreas, or biliary tract; GATA‑3 negativity rules out breast & bladder |
| Normal PET‑CT | Occult primary truly unknown; now classifies as ‘true CUP’ (≈15–20% of all CUP) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Young man (25 years) with a large mediastinal mass and elevated hCG** – initially looks like lymphoma or germ cell tumour; but biopsy shows carcinoma. Check AFP, β‑hCG, and PLAP; this is a favourable germ cell pattern.
- **Woman (55 years) with new‑onset ascites and omental cake on CT, no breast mass** – treat as occult ovarian cancer (serous papillary). IHC: CK7+, CK20–, WT‑1+, PAX8+, ER/PR positive. CA‑125 high. Primary is usually peritoneal or fallopian tube.
- **Man (70 years) with solitary lytic bone lesion in the femur and PSA 28** – bone biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. If IHC is PSA+ and NKX3.1+, treat as metastatic prostate cancer even if prostate exam normal. Do not label as CUP without PSA stain.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain‑language definition:**  
CUP is a cancer that has spread (metastasised) but the original tumour cannot be found despite thorough investigation. It is not one disease – it is a collection of clinical syndromes with different biology, treatment, and prognosis.

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** 3–5% of all cancers; as common as liver cancer or cervical cancer.  
- **Diagnostic challenge:** 80% of patients have an occult primary identified at autopsy, but pre‑mortem it remains hidden. The goal is to avoid futile searches while still finding treatable subsets.  
- **Stakes:** Median survival for ‘poor‑risk’ CUP is ~6 months; but 20–25% of patients fall into favourable subsets (e.g., germ cell, breast‑like, ovarian‑like) and can achieve long‑term remission or cure. Missing these subsets is a devastating error.  
- **Common pitfall:** Treating all CUP as a single entity with one chemotherapy regimen. Stratify by histology, IHC, and molecular profile.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**Story of development:**  
The primary tumour may have been eliminated by immune surveillance (T‑cell mediated destruction) or may have undergone spontaneous regression while micrometastases survived. Alternatively, the primary may be too small to detect (e.g., <5 mm in the lung or pancreas) or in a silent location (e.g., ampulla of Vater). The metastases themselves evolve and sometimes dedifferentiate, making lineage tracing difficult.

**Why each clinical sign happens:**

| Clinical Finding | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| Hepatomegaly | Metastases cause sinusoidal expansion, hepatic stretch, and capsule irritation |
| Supraclavicular node (Virchow’s) | Thoracic duct drainage from abdominal viscera; most often left‑sided |
| Weight loss / cachexia | Cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑6) from tumour and host immune response cause catabolism, anorexia |
| Elevated LDH | Tumour turnover, necrosis; also a marker of high tumour burden and poor prognosis |
| Deranged LFTs | Hepatic infiltration, bile duct compression → obstructive pattern |
| Hypercalcaemia | Paraneoplastic PTHrP (most often squamous or renal cell primaries) or bone lysis |
| Peritoneal carcinomatosis | Ovarian, gastric, or pancreatic primaries shed cells into peritoneal cavity; ascites from impaired lymphatic drainage |

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
Axillary node adenocarcinoma in a woman (pattern clue) occurs because axial lymphatics drain the breast; the primary is almost always occult invasive ductal carcinoma. Peritoneal mets in a woman (pattern clue) indicate either ovarian or fallopian tube origin – these primaries often remain small but exfoliate cells directly into peritoneum. Midline mediastinal or retroperitoneal masses in a young man (pattern clue) are often extragonadal germ cell tumours, arising from primitive germ cells that failed to migrate.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue That Points To It |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most CUP (≈80%) – no primary ever found | Normal PET‑CT, no prior cancer, no IHC lineage |
| **Lung** | Adenocarcinoma (most common occult primary) | Smoker, CK7+/TTF‑1+, Napsin A+ |
| **Pancreas / Biliary** | Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma | Painless jaundice, CA19‑9 high, CK7+/CK20–, SMAD4 loss |
| **Colorectal** | Colon adenocarcinoma | CK20+/CDX‑2+, CEA high, liver‑dominant mets |
| **Breast** | Invasive ductal carcinoma, often ER+/HER2– | Axillary nodes, biopsy GATA‑3+, ER+, Mammaglobin+ |
| **Ovary / Fallopian** | Serous carcinoma | Peritoneal spread, CA‑125 high, WT‑1+, PAX8+ |
| **Germ cell** | Testicular / extragonadal germ cell | Young male, midline mass, elevated AFP, β‑hCG, OCT3/4+, SALL4+ |
| **Prostate** | Adenocarcinoma | Diffuse bone mets, PSA high, NKX3.1+, AR+ |
| **Lymphoma** | Diffuse large B‑cell (rarely occult) | B symptoms, LDH very high, CD20+, MUM1+ |
| **Melanoma** | Occult primary (melanoma of unknown primary) | Subcutaneous or nodal mets with melanin pigment, S100+, HMB45+ |
| **Kidney** | Renal cell carcinoma | Haematuria, hypercalcaemia, clear cell morphology, PAX8+/CA9+ |
| **Head & Neck** | Squamous cell carcinoma | Solitary neck node (cystic), p16+ for HPV‑related occult tonsillar primary |

**Most common cause:** Lung adenocarcinoma (≈20% of CUP primaries found at autopsy).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
CUP is a diagnosis of exclusion. The **NCCN definition** requires:
1. Histologically confirmed metastasis (not lymphoma, sarcoma, germ cell, or melanoma by IHC)
2. No primary tumour identified after:
   - Thorough history & physical (including breast, pelvic, rectal, skin, lymphatic exam)
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
   - Targeted imaging as indicated (mammography, colonoscopy, PET‑CT)
   - Tumour markers

**Key investigations table:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** | Structural lesions, possible primary (lung mass, pancreatic mass, etc.) | Standard first‑line to identify primary or guide biopsy | No primary found → proceed to PET‑CT |
| **PET‑CT with FDG** | Metabolic hotspots | Detects occult primary in 30–40% of CUP; also stages all disease | Focal uptake in pharynx, tonsil, colon suggests primary; negative = true CUP |
| **Biopsy (core needle)** | Histology subtype (adeno, SCC, poorly diff) + IHC | Essential for lineage assignment | IHC panel: CK7, CK20, TTF‑1, GATA‑3, PAX8, CDX‑2, PSA, OCT3/4, S100 |
| **Tumour markers** | CEA, CA19‑9, CA‑125, AFP, β‑hCG, LDH, PSA | Support suspected primary; guide direction | CA‑125 >500 + WT‑1+ → ovarian; CEA+CDX‑2+ → colorectal; AFP/hCG ↑ → germ cell |
| **Mammography** | Occult breast cancer | **Mandatory** in women with axillary node adenocarcinoma, even if lump not felt | Suspicious microcalcifications → MRI breast; if MRI negative, IHC is crucial |
| **Colonoscopy** | Colonic primary | Only if PET‑CT shows colonic uptake or IHC is CK20+/CDX‑2+ | Polyp or mass found in <10% of CUP patients |
| **IHC (core panel)** | Protein expression pattern | Single best test to narrow primary source | See Pearls below for common patterns |
| **Gene expression profiling (CUP‑assay)** | 92‑gene signature | When IHC inconclusive (10–15% of cases) | Predicts primary site with ~90% accuracy; can guide site‑specific therapy |

**Pattern‑recognition: what single finding confirms or rules out**  
- **Confirms:** IHC showing TTF‑1+ in lung‑dominant mets → lung primary is virtually certain.  
- **Rules out:** Presence of a mass on PET‑CT that is biopsied and shows the same histology as the mets → now it’s not CUP, but metastatic cancer with known primary.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Assess and treat complications:**  
  - Hypercalcaemia (corrected Ca >2.6): IV fluids 200 mL/hr, then zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (if CrCl >35 mL/min).  
  - Spinal cord compression (back pain + leg weakness): dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat, urgent MRI whole spine.  
  - Massive pleural effusion with dyspnoea: therapeutic thoracentesis (remove 1–1.5 L) + send fluid for cytology and IHC.  
- **Analgesia:** Step 3 WHO ladder – morphine oral 5–10 mg q4h if severe pain.  
- **Admit to oncology ward or medical take** – begin diagnostic workup within hours.

**Ward (24–72 h):**
- **Confirm diagnosis with biopsy** – preferably from the most accessible metastasis (e.g., lymph node, liver). Send for:  
  - H&E (morphology: adeno, SCC, poorly diff, neuroendocrine)  
  - IHC panel: CK7, CK20, TTF‑1, GATA‑3, PAX8, CDX‑2, OCT3/4, PSA, S100  
  - If LDH >1.5× ULN or young male, also AFP and β‑hCG (serum)  
- **Risk stratification – identify favourable subsets:**
  - **Women with peritoneal serous carcinoma** → treat as advanced ovarian cancer: carboplatin AUC 5–6 + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² q3w × 6 cycles.  
  - **Men with bone metastases + PSA >10** → treat as metastatic prostate cancer: ADT (leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3m) ± docetaxel 75 mg/m² q3w × 6 cycles.  
  - **Young patients with midline tumour + elevated AFP/hCG** → treat as good‑risk germ cell: cisplatin 20 mg/m² + etoposide 100 mg/m² day 1–5 q3w × 4 cycles.  
  - **Solitary neck node, SCC, p16+** → treat as oropharyngeal cancer: definitive chemoradiation (cisplatin 100 mg/m² q3w × 2 cycles with RT 70 Gy).  
- **Unfavourable CUP** (majority): start empiric chemotherapy: **carboplatin AUC 5 + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV q3w** for 6 cycles. Alternatively, gemcitabine + cisplatin.  
- **Targeted therapy:** send next‑generation sequencing (NGS) for actionable mutations (NTRK fusions, MSI‑H, HER2, ROS1, ALK). If found, treat accordingly (e.g., larotrectinib 100 mg PO BID for NTRK fusion).

**Long‑term:**
- After 6 cycles, reassess with PET‑CT.  
  - Complete response → observation.  
  - Partial response → consider maintenance (e.g., gemcitabine maintenance if fit).  
  - Progression → switch to second‑line: FOLFIRI if no previous irinotecan, or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab 200 mg q3w for MSI‑H).  
- Prognosis counselling: median survival 6–12 mo for unfavourable, but 2–5 yr for favourable subsets.  
- Palliative care integration early – symptom burden is high.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Don’t start empiric chemotherapy before obtaining IHC and tumour markers** – you may be giving inappropriate drugs and miss a curable subset.  
- **Don’t treat all CUP with a single ‘CUP regimen’** – always stratify.  
- **Don’t assume a lung primary just because the patient smokes** – await IHC.  
- **Don’t give platinum agents to a patient with PSA‑positive bone‑only mets** – they need hormonal therapy, not cytotoxic chemotherapy.  
- **Don’t forget to check MSI and NTRK in all CUP** – these are rare but actionable.  
- **Don’t repeat extensive imaging repeatedly** – once PET‑CT and endoscopies are negative, further search is futile.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | CUP | Occult Primary Lung Cancer | Lymphoma (Diffuse Large B‑Cell) | Mesothelioma (Pleural) |
|---------|-----|-----------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain** | Varies by site (RUQ pain, bone pain) | Chest pain, dyspnoea | Painless lymphadenopathy, bone pain if involved | Unilateral pleuritic chest pain, dull ache |
| **Exam** | Hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy, ascites | Clubbing, haemoptysis, SVCO | Night sweats, fever, pruritus, splenomegaly | Pleural effusion, fixed chest |
| **ECG** | Normal | May show atrial fibrillation if SVCO | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | CEA, CA‑125, CA19‑9 may be elevated. LDH high. | LDH ± CEA | LDH very high (>2× ULN), β2‑microglobulin ↑ | Hyaluronic acid (fluid) ↑, LDH |
| **Key diff** | IHC shows lineage; no primary on PET‑CT | CT shows lung mass; IHC TTF‑1+ | Biopsy shows CD20+, no carcinoma IHC | Asbestos history, pleural thickening, IHC calretinin+, WT‑1+, CK5/6+ |

**Mimics to always exclude:**  
- **Tuberculosis** – systemic symptoms, positive IGRA, PET‑avid nodes, granulomas on biopsy (no malignant cells).  
- **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar nodes, elevated ACE, non‑caseating granulomas.  
- **Metastatic colorectal with unknown primary** – CK20+/CDX‑2+, can present with liver mets; colonoscopy may be negative, but IHC rules in.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hypercalcaemia of malignancy** | Confusion, constipation, polyuria, bone pain | Corrected Ca >2.75, ECG shortened QT | IV fluids 200 mL/hr, zoledronic acid 4 mg IV, ± calcitonin 4 U/kg SC q12h |
| **Spinal cord compression** | Back pain (often thoracic), leg weakness, urinary retention | Sensory level, reduced anal tone, lower limb hyperreflexia | Dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat, urgent MRI spine, radiotherapy or decompression within 24–48 h |
| **Pathological fracture** | Sudden pain, inability to weight‑bear, deformity | Tenderness, crepitus | Orthopaedic consult, radiology for prophylactic nailing if impending fracture (Mirels score ≥8) |
| **Malignant pleural effusion** | Dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, dry cough | Stony dullness, reduced breath sounds, bronchophony | Therapeutic thoracentesis (remove 1.5 L), send cytology; consider pleurodesis (talc slurry) if recurrent |
| **Venous thromboembolism** | Unilateral leg swelling, breathlessness, pleuritic pain | DVT signs, raised JVP, low SaO₂ | Start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily). CUP has a very high VTE risk – consider prophylactic anticoagulation if bedridden |
| **Liver failure** | Jaundice, confusion, easy bruising | Ascites, asterixis, elevated INR, bilirubin >50 | Supportive care; consider trans‑arterial embolisation if rapid growth of liver mets |
| **Bowel obstruction** | Abdominal distension, vomiting, constipation | Tympanic on percussion, high‑pitched bowel sounds | NG tube, IV fluids, CT abdomen, surgical consult if complete obstruction |

**Late presentation pattern (unfavourable CUP):**  
The patient presents 6‑month after symptom onset, now cachectic, with massive hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites, and multiple lung mets. ECOG performance status 3. LDH >1000, albumin <25. This is end‑stage. Median survival weeks. Focus on palliative care, avoid aggressive chemotherapy.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **“CUP” for workup:**  
   - **C** – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis  
   - **U** – Ultrasound (targeted, e.g., breast, testis)  
   - **P** – PET‑CT (if CT negative) and **P**athology (IHC)

2. **“PSBOG” for favourable CUP subsets:**  
   - **P** – Prostate (bone mets, PSA+)  
   - **S** – Squamous (neck node, p16+)  
   - **B** – Breast (axillary nodes, GATA‑3+)  
   - **O** – Ovary (peritoneal, CA‑125 high)  
   - **G** – Germ cell (young, midline, AFP/hCG)

3. **“TTIP” for unfavourable IHC patterns:**  
   - **T** – TTF‑1 negative  
   - **T** – Tobaco (lung should be #1 suspect)  
   - **I** – Indeterminate keratin pattern  
   - **P** – Poorly differentiated – gives worse prognosis

**Clinical pearls:**

- **Always biopsy the most accessible lesion** – don’t try to chase the primary. The biopsy tells you the biology.
- **Send IHC on the same block as H&E** – do not wait for H&E result; order a standard panel upfront.
- **In men, check PSA even if prostate feels normal** – occult prostate cancer can present as CUP.
- **In women with axillary nodes, do bilateral mammography and breast MRI** even if first imaging is normal; up to 10% will have occult breast cancer.
- **PET‑CT changes management in 40% of CUP** – it identifies the primary and also shows unsuspected metastases. Use it early.
- **Gene expression profiling (CUP‑assay) is not universally available but is strongly recommended if IHC is inconclusive** – it can identify a treatable primary site.
- **Do not start chemotherapy before knowing MSI/MMR status** – MSI‑high CUP responds very well to immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) and has a better prognosis.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Calling it CUP without complete IHC** – if you haven’t done TTF‑1, GATA‑3, CDX‑2, PAX8, it’s not yet CUP, it’s a work‑in‑progress.
- **Starting chemotherapy for a patient with bone‑only

## Cancer of Unknown Primary (CUP)

**One-liner:** Metastatic malignancy with no identifiable primary site after standard workup; ~3% of cancers, median survival ~6–12 months, but subsets are treatable.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old man presents with 4 weeks of progressive right upper quadrant pain, early satiety, and 5 kg weight loss. He has a 50‑pack‑year smoking history. Examination reveals hepatomegaly, mild jaundice, and a palpable left supraclavicular node. Bloods show deranged LFTs, LDH 850 U/L, and CEA 45 ng/mL. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrates multiple hypodense liver lesions, scattered lung nodules, and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy but no primary tumour. Biopsy of the liver lesion shows poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) is CK7+/CK20–, TTF‑1 negative, GATA‑3 negative. A PET‑CT reveals no occult primary.

**Dissect the vignette – what each clue tells you:**

| Clue | Pattern Interpretation |
|------|------------------------|
| Age >65, smoker | High risk for lung, pancreas, head & neck primaries |
| Hepatomegaly + left supraclavicular node | Distant metastases (Virchow’s node often left‑sided from gastrointestinal primaries) |
| Multiple liver & lung mets | Haematogenous spread; primary could be lung, pancreas, colorectal, or breast |
| Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma | Most common histology in CUP (≈50%); need IHC to narrow origin |
| CK7+/CK20–, TTF‑1–, GATA‑3– | Favours lung (but TTF‑1 negative argues against classic lung adenocarcinoma), pancreas, or biliary tract; GATA‑3 negativity rules out breast & bladder |
| Normal PET‑CT | Occult primary truly unknown; now classifies as ‘true CUP’ (≈15–20% of all CUP) |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

- **Young man (25 years) with a large mediastinal mass and elevated hCG** – initially looks like lymphoma or germ cell tumour; but biopsy shows carcinoma. Check AFP, β‑hCG, and PLAP; this is a favourable germ cell pattern.
- **Woman (55 years) with new‑onset ascites and omental cake on CT, no breast mass** – treat as occult ovarian cancer (serous papillary). IHC: CK7+, CK20–, WT‑1+, PAX8+, ER/PR positive. CA‑125 high. Primary is usually peritoneal or fallopian tube.
- **Man (70 years) with solitary lytic bone lesion in the femur and PSA 28** – bone biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. If IHC is PSA+ and NKX3.1+, treat as metastatic prostate cancer even if prostate exam normal. Do not label as CUP without PSA stain.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain‑language definition:**  
CUP is a cancer that has spread (metastasised) but the original tumour cannot be found despite thorough investigation. It is not one disease – it is a collection of clinical syndromes with different biology, treatment, and prognosis.

**Why it matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** 3–5% of all cancers; as common as liver cancer or cervical cancer.  
- **Diagnostic challenge:** 80% of patients have an occult primary identified at autopsy, but pre‑mortem it remains hidden. The goal is to avoid futile searches while still finding treatable subsets.  
- **Stakes:** Median survival for ‘poor‑risk’ CUP is ~6 months; but 20–25% of patients fall into favourable subsets (e.g., germ cell, breast‑like, ovarian‑like) and can achieve long‑term remission or cure. Missing these subsets is a devastating error.  
- **Common pitfall:** Treating all CUP as a single entity with one chemotherapy regimen. Stratify by histology, IHC, and molecular profile.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**Story of development:**  
The primary tumour may have been eliminated by immune surveillance (T‑cell mediated destruction) or may have undergone spontaneous regression while micrometastases survived. Alternatively, the primary may be too small to detect (e.g., <5 mm in the lung or pancreas) or in a silent location (e.g., ampulla of Vater). The metastases themselves evolve and sometimes dedifferentiate, making lineage tracing difficult.

**Why each clinical sign happens:**

| Clinical Finding | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| Hepatomegaly | Metastases cause sinusoidal expansion, hepatic stretch, and capsule irritation |
| Supraclavicular node (Virchow’s) | Thoracic duct drainage from abdominal viscera; most often left‑sided |
| Weight loss / cachexia | Cytokines (TNF‑α, IL‑6) from tumour and host immune response cause catabolism, anorexia |
| Elevated LDH | Tumour turnover, necrosis; also a marker of high tumour burden and poor prognosis |
| Deranged LFTs | Hepatic infiltration, bile duct compression → obstructive pattern |
| Hypercalcaemia | Paraneoplastic PTHrP (most often squamous or renal cell primaries) or bone lysis |
| Peritoneal carcinomatosis | Ovarian, gastric, or pancreatic primaries shed cells into peritoneal cavity; ascites from impaired lymphatic drainage |

**Connection to pattern recognition:**  
Axillary node adenocarcinoma in a woman (pattern clue) occurs because axial lymphatics drain the breast; the primary is almost always occult invasive ductal carcinoma. Peritoneal mets in a woman (pattern clue) indicate either ovarian or fallopian tube origin – these primaries often remain small but exfoliate cells directly into peritoneum. Midline mediastinal or retroperitoneal masses in a young man (pattern clue) are often extragonadal germ cell tumours, arising from primitive germ cells that failed to migrate.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue That Points To It |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------|
| **Idiopathic** | Most CUP (≈80%) – no primary ever found | Normal PET‑CT, no prior cancer, no IHC lineage |
| **Lung** | Adenocarcinoma (most common occult primary) | Smoker, CK7+/TTF‑1+, Napsin A+ |
| **Pancreas / Biliary** | Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma | Painless jaundice, CA19‑9 high, CK7+/CK20–, SMAD4 loss |
| **Colorectal** | Colon adenocarcinoma | CK20+/CDX‑2+, CEA high, liver‑dominant mets |
| **Breast** | Invasive ductal carcinoma, often ER+/HER2– | Axillary nodes, biopsy GATA‑3+, ER+, Mammaglobin+ |
| **Ovary / Fallopian** | Serous carcinoma | Peritoneal spread, CA‑125 high, WT‑1+, PAX8+ |
| **Germ cell** | Testicular / extragonadal germ cell | Young male, midline mass, elevated AFP, β‑hCG, OCT3/4+, SALL4+ |
| **Prostate** | Adenocarcinoma | Diffuse bone mets, PSA high, NKX3.1+, AR+ |
| **Lymphoma** | Diffuse large B‑cell (rarely occult) | B symptoms, LDH very high, CD20+, MUM1+ |
| **Melanoma** | Occult primary (melanoma of unknown primary) | Subcutaneous or nodal mets with melanin pigment, S100+, HMB45+ |
| **Kidney** | Renal cell carcinoma | Haematuria, hypercalcaemia, clear cell morphology, PAX8+/CA9+ |
| **Head & Neck** | Squamous cell carcinoma | Solitary neck node (cystic), p16+ for HPV‑related occult tonsillar primary |

**Most common cause:** Lung adenocarcinoma (≈20% of CUP primaries found at autopsy).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
CUP is a diagnosis of exclusion. The **NCCN definition** requires:
1. Histologically confirmed metastasis (not lymphoma, sarcoma, germ cell, or melanoma by IHC)
2. No primary tumour identified after:
   - Thorough history & physical (including breast, pelvic, rectal, skin, lymphatic exam)
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
   - Targeted imaging as indicated (mammography, colonoscopy, PET‑CT)

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** | Structural lesions, possible primary (lung mass, pancreatic mass, etc.) | Standard first‑line to identify primary or guide biopsy | No primary found → proceed to PET‑CT |
| **PET‑CT with FDG** | Metabolic hotspots | Detects occult primary in 30–40% of CUP; also stages all disease | Focal uptake in pharynx, tonsil, colon suggests primary; negative = true CUP |
| **Biopsy (core needle)** | Histology subtype (adeno, SCC, poorly diff) + IHC | Essential for lineage assignment | IHC panel: CK7, CK20, TTF‑1, GATA‑3, PAX8, CDX‑2, PSA, OCT3/4, S100 |
| **Tumour markers** | CEA, CA19‑9, CA‑125, AFP, β‑hCG, LDH, PSA | Support suspected primary; guide direction | CA‑125 >500 + WT‑1+ → ovarian; CEA+CDX‑2+ → colorectal; AFP/hCG ↑ → germ cell |
| **Mammography** | Occult breast cancer | **Mandatory** in women with axillary node adenocarcinoma, even if lump not felt | Suspicious microcalcifications → MRI breast; if MRI negative, IHC is crucial |
| **Colonoscopy** | Colonic primary | Only if PET‑CT shows colonic uptake or IHC is CK20+/CDX‑2+ | Polyp or mass found in <10% of CUP patients |
| **IHC (core panel)** | Protein expression pattern | Single best test to narrow primary source | See Pearls below for common patterns |
| **Gene expression profiling (CUP‑assay)** | 92‑gene signature | When IHC inconclusive (10–15% of cases) | Predicts primary site with ~90% accuracy; can guide site‑specific therapy |

**Pattern‑recognition: what single finding confirms or rules out**  
- **Confirms:** IHC showing TTF‑1+ in lung‑dominant mets → lung primary is virtually certain.  
- **Rules out:** Presence of a mass on PET‑CT that is biopsied and shows the same histology as the mets → now it’s not CUP, but metastatic cancer with known primary.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Assess and treat complications:**  
  - Hypercalcaemia (corrected Ca >2.6): IV fluids 200 mL/hr, then zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (if CrCl >35 mL/min).  
  - Spinal cord compression (back pain + leg weakness): dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat, urgent MRI whole spine.  
  - Massive pleural effusion with dyspnoea: therapeutic thoracentesis (remove 1–1.5 L) + send fluid for cytology and IHC.  
- **Analgesia:** Step 3 WHO ladder – morphine oral 5–10 mg q4h if severe pain.

**Ward (24–72 h):**
- **Confirm diagnosis with biopsy** – preferably from the most accessible metastasis (e.g., lymph node, liver). Send for:  
  - H&E (morphology: adeno, SCC, poorly diff, neuroendocrine)  
  - IHC panel: CK7, CK20, TTF‑1, GATA‑3, PAX8, CDX‑2, OCT3/4, PSA, S100  
  - If LDH >1.5× ULN or young male, also AFP and β‑hCG (serum)  
- **Risk stratification – identify favourable subsets:**
  - **Women with peritoneal serous carcinoma** → treat as advanced ovarian cancer: carboplatin AUC 5–6 + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² q3w × 6 cycles.  
  - **Men with bone metastases + PSA >10** → treat as metastatic prostate cancer: ADT (leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3m) ± docetaxel 75 mg/m² q3w × 6 cycles.  
  - **Young patients with midline tumour + elevated AFP/hCG** → treat as good‑risk germ cell: cisplatin 20 mg/m² + etoposide 100 mg/m² day 1–5 q3w × 4 cycles.  
  - **Solitary neck node, SCC, p16+** → treat as oropharyngeal cancer: definitive chemoradiation (cisplatin 100 mg/m² q3w × 2 cycles with RT 70 Gy).  
- **Unfavourable CUP** (majority): start empiric chemotherapy: **carboplatin AUC 5 + paclitaxel 175 mg/m² IV q3w** for 6 cycles. Alternatively, gemcitabine + cisplatin.  
- **Targeted therapy:** send next‑generation sequencing (NGS) for actionable mutations (NTRK fusions, MSI‑H, HER2, ROS1, ALK). If found, treat accordingly (e.g., larotrectinib 100 mg PO BID for NTRK fusion).

**Long‑term:**
- After 6 cycles, reassess with PET‑CT.  
  - Complete response → observation.  
  - Partial response → consider maintenance (e.g., gemcitabine maintenance if fit).  
  - Progression → switch to second‑line: FOLFIRI if no previous irinotecan, or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab 200 mg q3w for MSI‑H).  
- Prognosis counselling: median survival 6–12 mo for unfavourable, but 2–5 yr for favourable subsets.  
- Palliative care integration early – symptom burden is high.

**What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
- **Don’t start empiric chemotherapy before obtaining IHC and tumour markers** – you may be giving inappropriate drugs and miss a curable subset.  
- **Don’t treat all CUP with a single ‘CUP regimen’** – always stratify.  
- **Don’t assume a lung primary just because the patient smokes** – await IHC.  
- **Don’t give platinum agents to a patient with PSA‑positive bone‑only mets** – they need hormonal therapy, not cytotoxic chemotherapy.  
- **Don’t forget to check MSI and NTRK in all CUP** – these are rare but actionable.  
- **Don’t repeat extensive imaging repeatedly** – once PET‑CT and endoscopies are negative, further search is futile.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | CUP | Occult Primary Lung Cancer | Lymphoma (Diffuse Large B‑Cell) | Mesothelioma (Pleural) |
|---------|-----|-----------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Pain** | Varies by site (RUQ pain, bone pain) | Chest pain, dyspnoea | Painless lymphadenopathy, bone pain if involved | Unilateral pleuritic chest pain, dull ache |
| **Exam** | Hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy, ascites | Clubbing, haemoptysis, SVCO | Night sweats, fever, pruritus, splenomegaly | Pleural effusion, fixed chest |
| **ECG** | Normal | May show atrial fibrillation if SVCO | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | CEA, CA‑125, CA19‑9 may be elevated. LDH high. | LDH ± CEA | LDH very high (>2× ULN), β2‑microglobulin ↑ | Hyaluronic acid (fluid) ↑, LDH |
| **Key diff** | IHC shows lineage; no primary on PET‑CT | CT shows lung mass; IHC TTF‑1+ | Biopsy shows CD20+, no carcinoma IHC | Asbestos history, pleural thickening, IHC calretinin+, WT‑1+, CK5/6+ |

**Mimics to always exclude:**  
- **Tuberculosis** – systemic symptoms, positive IGRA, PET‑avid nodes, granulomas on biopsy (no malignant cells).  
- **Sarcoidosis** – bilateral hilar nodes, elevated ACE, non‑caseating granulomas.  
- **Metastatic colorectal with unknown primary** – CK20+/CDX‑2+, can present with liver mets; colonoscopy may be negative, but IHC rules in.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Hypercalcaemia of malignancy** | Confusion, constipation, polyuria, bone pain | Corrected Ca >2.75, ECG shortened QT | IV fluids 200 mL/hr, zoledronic acid 4 mg IV, ± calcitonin 4 U/kg SC q12h |
| **Spinal cord compression** | Back pain (often thoracic), leg weakness, urinary retention | Sensory level, reduced anal tone, lower limb hyperreflexia | Dexamethasone 10 mg IV stat, urgent MRI spine, radiotherapy or decompression within 24–48 h |
| **Pathological fracture** | Sudden pain, inability to weight‑bear, deformity | Tenderness, crepitus | Orthopaedic consult, radiology for prophylactic nailing if impending fracture (Mirels score ≥8) |
| **Malignant pleural effusion** | Dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, dry cough | Stony dullness, reduced breath sounds, bronchophony | Therapeutic thoracentesis (remove 1.5 L), send cytology; consider pleurodesis (talc slurry) if recurrent |
| **Venous thromboembolism** | Unilateral leg swelling, breathlessness, pleuritic pain | DVT signs, raised JVP, low SaO₂ | Start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily). CUP has a very high VTE risk – consider prophylactic anticoagulation if bedridden |
| **Liver failure** | Jaundice, confusion, easy bruising | Ascites, asterixis, elevated INR, bilirubin >50 | Supportive care; consider trans‑arterial embolisation if rapid growth of liver mets |
| **Bowel obstruction** | Abdominal distension, vomiting, constipation | Tympanic on percussion, high‑pitched bowel sounds | NG tube, IV fluids, CT abdomen, surgical consult if complete obstruction |

**Late presentation pattern (unfavourable CUP):**  
The patient presents 6‑month after symptom onset, now cachectic, with massive hepatomegaly, jaundice, ascites, and multiple lung mets. ECOG performance status 3. LDH >1000, albumin <25. This is end‑stage. Median survival weeks. Focus on palliative care, avoid aggressive chemotherapy.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **“CUP” for workup:**  
   - **C** – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis  
   - **U** – Ultrasound (targeted, e.g., breast, testis)  
   - **P** – PET‑CT (if CT negative) and **P**athology (IHC)

2. **“PSBOG” for favourable CUP subsets:**  
   - **P** – Prostate (bone mets, PSA+)  
   - **S** – Squamous (neck node, p16+)  
   - **B** – Breast (axillary nodes, GATA‑3+)  
   - **O** – Ovary (peritoneal, CA‑125 high)  
   - **G** – Germ cell (young, midline, AFP/hCG)

3. **“TTIP” for unfavourable IHC patterns:**  
   - **T** – TTF‑1 negative  
   - **T** – Tobaco (lung should be #1 suspect)  
   - **I** – Indeterminate keratin pattern  
   - **P** – Poorly differentiated – gives worse prognosis

**Clinical pearls:**

- **Always biopsy the most accessible lesion** – don’t try to chase the primary. The biopsy tells you the biology.
- **Send IHC on the same block as H&E** – do not wait for H&E result; order a standard panel upfront.
- **In men, check PSA even if prostate feels normal** – occult prostate cancer can present as CUP.
- **In women with axillary nodes, do bilateral mammography and breast MRI** even if first imaging is normal; up to 10% will have occult breast cancer.
- **PET‑CT changes management in 40% of CUP** – it identifies the primary and also shows unsuspected metastases. Use it early.
- **Gene expression profiling (CUP‑assay) is not universally available but is strongly recommended if IHC is inconclusive** – it can identify a treatable primary site.
- **Do not start chemotherapy before knowing MSI/MMR status** – MSI‑high CUP responds very well to immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) and has a better prognosis.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Calling it CUP without complete IHC** – if you haven’t done TTF‑1, GATA‑3, CDX‑2, PAX8, it’s not yet CUP, it’s a work‑in‑progress.
- **Starting chemotherapy for a patient with bone‑only
